courses.course_description: Discover the rise and fall of the largest contiguous land empire in history — from Genghis Khan's unification of the steppe tribes to the empire's vast cultural and trade legacy.

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 1: The Rise of Genghis Khan
chapters.chapter_desc: How a boy from the harsh Mongolian steppe united warring tribes and founded the world's greatest empire.

chapter_content.content:
It is the year 1162 on the windswept plains of Mongolia. A child is born clutching a blood clot in his fist — a sign, the shamans say, of a great warrior. His name is Temüjin.

His early years are brutal. When Temüjin is just nine, his father is poisoned by rival Tatars. His family is abandoned by their own clan and left to survive alone in the wilderness. But hardship forges iron.

As a young man, Temüjin is kidnapped, enslaved, and nearly broken. Yet each setback makes him more resourceful, more relentless. He builds alliances through loyalty rather than birthright — an unusual idea on the steppe, where blood lineage ruled everything.

By 1206, after decades of war, diplomacy, and sheer force of will, Temüjin has done the impossible: he has unified all the Mongol and Turkic tribes under a single banner. At a grand assembly called the kurultai, he is proclaimed Genghis Khan — 'Universal Ruler'.

His new nation has no cities, no written language, and no standing walls. But it has something more dangerous: an army of 100,000 disciplined, mobile, and utterly dedicated warriors — and a leader who had already survived everything the world could throw at him.

chapter_content.image_alt: Map showing the unification of Mongol tribes across the Central Asian steppe circa 1206

chapter_content.title: Genghis Khan and the Mongol Empire – History Documentary

questions.question_content: What was Genghis Khan's birth name?
In which year was Genghis Khan proclaimed 'Universal Ruler' at the kurultai assembly?
What made Genghis Khan's approach to building alliances unusual on the Mongolian steppe?

answers.answer_content: He relied on blood lineage above all else
He built alliances based on loyalty rather than birthright
He bought loyalty with gold and silver
He only allied with tribes that shared his language

questions.question_content: Approximately how large was Genghis Khan's army after unifying the tribes?

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 2: Conquest and the Pax Mongolica
chapters.chapter_desc: How Mongol expansion devastated empires — yet paradoxically connected the world through the greatest free-trade zone in history.

chapter_content.content:
Imagine you are a merchant in Samarkand in 1218. Your city is the jewel of the Silk Road — prosperous, cultured, protected by massive walls. Then a message arrives from the east: the Mongols are coming.

Genghis Khan had sent a trade caravan of 450 merchants to the Khwarazmian Empire. The governor executed them all. This was perhaps the costliest mistake in history.

The Mongol revenge was apocalyptic. City after city fell. Samarkand, Merv, Nishapur — populations numbered in the hundreds of thousands were wiped out.

And yet — here is the paradox — after the conquest came something extraordinary. Under Kublai Khan, Genghis's grandson, the empire at its peak stretched from Korea to Poland, from Siberia to Persia. For the first time, a single political authority governed most of Eurasia.

This period is called the Pax Mongolica — the Mongol Peace. A merchant could travel from Venice to Beijing with a paiza (a Mongol passport tablet) and face no border. Ideas, technologies, and diseases moved freely. Gunpowder, printing, and the bubonic plague all reached Europe via Mongol-controlled routes.

Marco Polo made his famous journey during this era. The Mongols, destroyers of worlds, had accidentally created the first globalized network.

chapter_content.image_alt: Map of the Mongol Empire at its greatest extent in 1279, showing the four major khanates and Silk Road trade routes

chapter_content.title: Pax Mongolica: The Peaceful Legacy of a Brutal Empire

questions.question_content: What triggered the Mongol invasion of the Khwarazmian Empire?

answers.answer_content: The Khwarazmians attacked a Mongol border fortress
A Khwarazmian governor executed 450 Mongol trade merchants
The Khwarazmians allied with rival Tatar tribes
Genghis Khan sought to control the Silk Road's eastern end

questions.question_content: What was a paiza in the Mongol Empire?

answers.answer_content: A Mongol weapon used in siege warfare
A gold currency used along the Silk Road
A passport tablet that allowed safe travel across the empire
A military rank in the Mongol army

questions.question_content: What term describes the period of relative stability and open trade across the Mongol Empire?
Which famous European traveller journeyed to Asia during the Pax Mongolica?

courses.course_description: Explore the often-overlooked stories of women who shaped computing, space exploration, and digital innovation — from Ada Lovelace to the programmers of the Apollo mission.

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 1: The First Programmers Were Women
chapters.chapter_desc: Long before 'computer' meant a machine, it meant a person — and most of those persons were women.

chapter_content.content:
Picture the year 1843. Charles Babbage, a brilliant English mathematician, is obsessed with building a mechanical calculating machine — his 'Analytical Engine.' Almost no one understands what he is trying to do.

Except one person: Ada Lovelace, daughter of the poet Lord Byron.

Ada translates an Italian article about Babbage's machine and adds her own notes — three times longer than the original. Inside them, she describes something Babbage himself had not fully articulated: that the machine could be programmed to do any calculation, not just arithmetic. She writes what many historians consider the world's first computer algorithm — in 1843, a century before the first electronic computer.

Fast-forward to 1945. The US Army has built ENIAC, the world's first general-purpose electronic computer. It needs to be programmed. Six women are hired: Jean Jennings, Frances Bilas, Betty Snyder, Marlyn Wescoff, Ruth Lichterman, and Frances Elizalde. Working from logical diagrams with no manuals and no precedent, they make ENIAC calculate ballistic trajectories. They are called the ENIAC Programmers — and for decades, their contribution is almost completely forgotten.

This is a pattern that repeats throughout the history of computing: women do foundational work, men receive the recognition.

chapter_content.image_alt: The six ENIAC programmers operating the machine in 1945 at the University of Pennsylvania

chapter_content.title: The Secret History of The ENIAC Women | TEDx

questions.question_content: What is Ada Lovelace historically credited with writing?

answers.answer_content: The first textbook on electrical engineering

questions.question_content: What was Ada Lovelace's key insight about Babbage's Analytical Engine?

answers.answer_content: That it could only calculate artillery trajectories
That it could be programmed to perform any calculation, not just arithmetic
That it was too expensive to build
That it needed electrical power to function

questions.question_content: How many women were hired to program the ENIAC computer in 1945?
What task were the ENIAC programmers solving?

answers.answer_content: Calculating ballistic trajectories for the US Army

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 2: Hidden Figures and the Space Race
chapters.chapter_desc: How Black women mathematicians at NASA calculated the trajectories that sent humans into space — and how their story was hidden for half a century.

chapter_content.content:
It is 1961. NASA is in a desperate race against the Soviet Union to put a human in space. In a segregated building at Langley Research Center in Virginia, a group of African American women are doing the calculations that will determine whether astronauts live or die. They are called 'computers' — human computers — and among them is Katherine Johnson.

Katherine's mathematical intuition is so precise that when NASA begins using electronic IBM computers, astronaut John Glenn refuses to board his spacecraft until Katherine personally verifies the machine's calculations. 'If she says they're good,' Glenn reportedly says, 'then I'm ready to go.'

On February 20, 1962, Glenn orbits the Earth three times. The math holds.

Katherine's colleagues Dorothy Vaughan and Mary Jackson are equally extraordinary. Dorothy teaches herself FORTRAN and transitions her entire group into the new era of electronic computing, becoming NASA's first Black supervisor. Mary becomes NASA's first Black female engineer, fighting through legal barriers to attend the all-white night school courses she needed.

Their stories were largely unknown until the 2016 film Hidden Figures. In 2015, at age 97, Katherine Johnson received the Presidential Medal of Freedom.

chapter_content.image_alt: Katherine Johnson at her desk at NASA Langley Research Center, working on calculations in the 1960s

chapter_content.title: Hidden Figures Explained: The Women Who Out-Mathed NASA

questions.question_content: Why did astronaut John Glenn insist on Katherine Johnson verifying NASA's computer calculations?

answers.answer_content: She had personally built the IBM computer
He trusted her mathematical precision more than the electronic computer
NASA regulations required a human co-signer on all calculations
The IBM computer had broken down before his launch

questions.question_content: What milestone did Dorothy Vaughan achieve at NASA?

answers.answer_content: She became the first woman to walk in space
She became NASA's first Black supervisor
She designed the Apollo spacecraft heat shield
She became the first female astronaut candidate

questions.question_content: What programming language did Dorothy Vaughan teach herself and her team?
What prestigious award did Katherine Johnson receive in 2015?

courses.course_description: Journey through 14th–17th century Europe as art, science, and philosophy are reborn — tracing the revolution that transformed how humans see themselves and their world.

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 1: Why Florence? The Birth of a New World
chapters.chapter_desc: Why did one Italian city become the cradle of a cultural revolution that changed Western civilization forever?

chapter_content.content:
The year is 1347. A plague ship docks in Sicily. Within five years, the Black Death will kill between 30 and 60 percent of Europe's population. The Church cannot explain why the pious are dying alongside the sinful.

From this catastrophe, something unexpected emerges: a hunger for human answers.

In the wealthy city of Florence, a new generation of thinkers, artists, and merchants begins asking questions that would have seemed heretical to their grandparents: What can people achieve? How do we see, measure, and understand the world on its own terms?

The Medici family — bankers who have become the most powerful dynasty in Europe — believe that investing in artists and scholars is both spiritually valuable and politically shrewd. They fund everything.

A sculptor named Donatello creates David — the first free-standing nude sculpture since antiquity. An architect named Brunelleschi solves the 'impossible' problem of building a dome over Florence's cathedral without scaffolding by reinventing geometry. A writer named Petrarch digs up ancient Roman manuscripts and argues that Greek and Roman civilization represents a peak of human achievement worth reviving.

They call this revival the Rinascimento — the Rebirth. We call it the Renaissance. The radical idea at its heart: humans are not just sinners waiting to die — they are creators, capable of beauty, reason, and mastery of nature.

chapter_content.image_alt: Brunelleschi's dome over the Florence Cathedral (Duomo), completed in 1436

chapter_content.title: The Medici: Godfathers of the Renaissance – Birth of a Dynasty | PBS

questions.question_content: What catastrophic event preceded the Renaissance and shook European confidence in the Church?
Which powerful family funded Renaissance art and scholarship in Florence?
What did Brunelleschi achieve that was considered 'impossible' at the time?

answers.answer_content: He built the first stone bridge across the Arno river
He built a dome over Florence's cathedral without scaffolding
He carved the first life-size marble statue since antiquity
He designed the first printed book in Italian

questions.question_content: What does the Italian word 'Rinascimento' mean?

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 2: Leonardo, Michelangelo, and the Universal Person
chapters.chapter_desc: The Renaissance ideal of the 'uomo universale' and the two men who came closest to achieving it.

chapter_content.content:
The Renaissance invented a new kind of human ideal: the uomo universale — the universal man. Not a specialist, but a person who could paint, sculpt, write poetry, compose music, understand anatomy, design buildings, and theorize about philosophy — all equally well.

Leonardo da Vinci (1452–1519) is perhaps history's most curious mind. His 7,000 surviving notebook pages contain the first accurate drawings of the human heart's chambers, designs for a rudimentary helicopter and solar power, studies of water flow, and sketches for some of the most famous paintings ever created. He filled pages with mirror-writing, jumping between botany, geology, mathematics, and optics within a single entry. He also left dozens of projects unfinished — the Mona Lisa took 16 years.

Michelangelo (1475–1564) was almost his opposite: monomaniacally dedicated, tormented, devout. He insisted he was a sculptor above all else, yet the Sistine Chapel ceiling — painted lying on his back over four years — is arguably the greatest single artistic achievement in Western history. His David, Pietà, and the architectural design of St. Peter's Basilica span an unmatched range.

Both men lived into old age, both argued fiercely, and both died convinced they had not yet done their best work.

chapter_content.title: The Renaissance – The Age of Michelangelo and Leonardo da Vinci | DW Documentary

questions.question_content: What does the Renaissance term 'uomo universale' mean?

answers.answer_content: Universal man — a person who masters many fields

questions.question_content: Approximately how many pages of notebooks did Leonardo da Vinci leave behind?
How long did it take Michelangelo to paint the Sistine Chapel ceiling?
How long did Leonardo reportedly work on the Mona Lisa?

courses.course_description: Understand the science, history, and human stakes of Earth's changing climate — from the greenhouse effect to global tipping points.

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 1: The Greenhouse Effect — A Story 200 Years in the Making
chapters.chapter_desc: The science of climate change was discovered by 19th-century researchers. Here is how we came to understand what we are doing to our planet.

chapter_content.content:
It is 1856. A scientist named Eunice Newton Foote places two glass cylinders in sunlight — one filled with regular air, one filled with carbon dioxide. The CO₂ cylinder heats up much faster and stays hot much longer. She publishes a paper concluding that 'an atmosphere of that gas would give to our earth a high temperature.'

Her paper is presented at a scientific conference — by a man, because women are not allowed to present. It is then largely ignored for over a century.

Three years later, Irish physicist John Tyndall conducts similar experiments and receives full credit for discovering the greenhouse effect. In 1896, Swedish chemist Svante Arrhenius calculates that doubling atmospheric CO₂ would raise global temperatures by 5–6°C — remarkably close to modern estimates.

How does the greenhouse effect work? Think of Earth wrapped in a thin blanket of gases — mostly nitrogen and oxygen, but with small amounts of CO₂, methane, and water vapor. Sunlight passes through easily. But when the warmed Earth tries to radiate heat back into space, these 'greenhouse gases' trap some of it — like a car on a sunny day.

Without any greenhouse effect, Earth would be about -18°C. The natural effect makes life possible. The problem: since the Industrial Revolution, humans have been thickening that blanket. Since 1850, atmospheric CO₂ has risen from 280 to over 420 parts per million — levels not seen for at least 3 million years.

chapter_content.image_alt: Diagram illustrating the greenhouse effect: solar radiation entering Earth's atmosphere and heat being trapped by greenhouse gases

questions.question_content: Who conducted the first experiment linking CO₂ to atmospheric warming in 1856?
What would Earth's average temperature be WITHOUT the natural greenhouse effect?
What was the approximate atmospheric CO₂ level before the Industrial Revolution?
How long ago were today's CO₂ levels last seen on Earth?

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 2: Tipping Points — What Happens If We Don't Act
chapters.chapter_desc: Climate tipping points are thresholds beyond which change becomes self-sustaining and irreversible.

chapter_content.content:
Imagine pushing a rocking chair. You can push it harder and harder, and it rocks back and forth — but it returns to its starting position. Then, at some critical angle, it tips over. There is no coming back.

Climate scientists use the term 'tipping point' to describe moments when parts of Earth's system behave like that rocking chair: once a threshold is crossed, change becomes self-reinforcing and potentially irreversible on human timescales.

Three of the most critical:

1. The Greenland Ice Sheet. If it melts completely, global sea levels rise by about 7 meters. Miami, Amsterdam, Shanghai, and most of Bangladesh would be uninhabitable. Scientists estimate we may be approaching this tipping point at just 1.5–2°C of warming.

2. Amazon Rainforest Dieback. The Amazon generates much of its own rainfall. As deforestation reduces the forest, less rain falls — causing more drought, causing more trees to die. The world's largest tropical forest could transition to dry savanna, releasing decades' worth of global CO₂ emissions.

3. Permafrost Thaw. Siberia and northern Canada contain vast amounts of frozen organic matter. As it thaws, it releases methane — a greenhouse gas 80 times more potent than CO₂ over 20 years. This could trigger warming far beyond human emissions alone.

The alarming discovery: these tipping points may be interconnected — crossing one could trigger others in a cascade.

chapter_content.image_alt: World map showing major climate tipping points including Arctic sea ice, Greenland ice sheet, Amazon rainforest, and Atlantic circulation

chapter_content.title: The Tipping Point | Climate Change: The Facts | BBC Earth

questions.question_content: By how much would global sea levels rise if the Greenland Ice Sheet melted completely?
Why is Amazon deforestation considered a potential tipping point?

answers.answer_content: Because it releases oxygen that warms the atmosphere
Because reduced forest leads to less rainfall, causing further die-off in a self-reinforcing loop
Because it increases soil erosion that clogs major rivers
Because deforested land reflects too much sunlight

questions.question_content: How much more potent is methane than CO₂ as a greenhouse gas over 20 years?
At what level of global warming might the Greenland Ice Sheet tipping point be reached?

courses.course_description: Explore four decades of ideological conflict, nuclear brinkmanship, proxy wars, and cultural espionage that divided the world into two hostile camps.

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 1: From Allies to Enemies — How the Cold War Began
chapters.chapter_desc: In 1945, the US and USSR had just defeated Hitler together. Within three years, they were pointing nuclear weapons at each other.

chapter_content.content:
May 8, 1945. Nazi Germany surrenders. In cities across the Allied world — New York, London, Moscow — crowds pour into the streets in delirious celebration. The most destructive war in human history is over. Surely the alliance that won it will hold.

It will not last three years.

The cracks were always there, hidden beneath wartime necessity. The US wanted open markets, democratic governments, and a rule-based international order. The USSR wanted a buffer zone of friendly communist states on its western border — it had been invaded from the west in 1812, 1914, and 1941. It would not happen again.

At the Yalta Conference in February 1945, Roosevelt, Churchill, and Stalin carved up post-war Europe with vague promises about 'free elections.' By 1947, Soviet-backed communist governments had taken power in Poland, Hungary, Romania, and Bulgaria. The Iron Curtain — a phrase Churchill coined in a famous speech — had descended across Europe.

On the American side, diplomat George Kennan wrote an 8,000-word telegram from Moscow in 1946 that became the intellectual foundation of US Cold War policy: the USSR was expansionist by nature and could only be contained, not negotiated with.

And so the world divided. Two superpowers, two ideologies, two growing nuclear arsenals. A 'hot' war between them would mean the end of civilization. So instead, they fought everywhere else.

chapter_content.image_alt: Map of Europe in 1949 showing NATO members and Soviet-aligned Warsaw Pact countries with the Iron Curtain marked

chapter_content.title: 1945–1953: From Allied Unity to Cold War Divisions | Full Documentary

questions.question_content: What term did Churchill use to describe the division between Soviet-controlled Eastern Europe and the West?
What was the core idea of George Kennan's 1946 'Long Telegram'?

answers.answer_content: That the USSR should be invaded before it built nuclear weapons
That the USSR was expansionist and needed to be contained rather than negotiated with
That economic aid to Europe would prevent communism from spreading
That the US and USSR could form a permanent alliance against China

questions.question_content: How many times had Russia/USSR been invaded from the west before 1945?
At which wartime conference did Allied leaders try to agree on the post-war order in Europe?

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 2: The Cuban Missile Crisis — 13 Days That Almost Ended the World
chapters.chapter_desc: In October 1962, the world came closer to nuclear war than at any other point in history. What happened — and what saved us?

chapter_content.content:
October 16, 1962, 8:45 AM. President Kennedy is handed aerial reconnaissance photographs showing Soviet nuclear missile launch sites under construction in Cuba — 90 miles from Florida. The missiles can reach Washington DC in 13 minutes.

Kennedy's advisors are split. Military generals push for immediate air strikes. Others demand a naval blockade. A faction argues for secret back-channel diplomacy.

For 13 days, the world holds its breath. US warships encircle Cuba. Soviet cargo ships steam toward them. Nuclear bombers stay in the air around the clock. Military commanders on both sides have authority to launch under certain conditions.

Then, on October 27 — 'Black Saturday' — a US spy plane is shot down over Cuba, an American submarine is nearly destroyed by a Soviet depth charge, and a Soviet officer named Vasili Arkhipov refuses to authorize a nuclear torpedo strike despite believing his submarine was under attack. He may have saved the world.

A secret deal is struck: the USSR removes its Cuban missiles; the US secretly removes its missiles from Turkey and promises never to invade Cuba. Both sides compromise. Both claim victory.

The lesson: in a crisis, the most dangerous enemy is not the opponent — it is miscommunication, miscalculation, and the pressure to seem strong.

chapter_content.image_alt: US reconnaissance photograph from October 1962 showing Soviet nuclear missile launch pads under construction in Cuba

chapter_content.title: The Cuban Missile Crisis | Full Documentary

questions.question_content: How far from the US coast was Cuba, where Soviet missiles were being installed?
What did Soviet officer Vasili Arkhipov do on 'Black Saturday' that may have prevented nuclear war?

answers.answer_content: He secretly contacted Kennedy with peace terms
He refused to authorize a nuclear torpedo strike despite believing his submarine was under attack
He ordered Soviet ships to turn back from Cuba
He destroyed the Cuban missile launch codes

questions.question_content: What was the secret US concession in the deal that ended the Cuban Missile Crisis?

answers.answer_content: The US agreed to recognize Cuba as a Soviet territory
The US secretly removed its missiles from Turkey and promised not to invade Cuba
The US agreed to reduce its nuclear arsenal by half
The US withdrew all troops from Western Europe

questions.question_content: How many days did the Cuban Missile Crisis last?

courses.course_description: Uncover 3,000 years of one of humanity's most durable civilizations — from the unification of the Nile Valley to pharaohs and pyramids.

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 1: The Gift of the Nile — How a River Built a Civilization
chapters.chapter_desc: Why did Egypt succeed so spectacularly? The answer is a river, a flood cycle, and the genius to harness both.

chapter_content.content:
The Greek historian Herodotus described it simply: 'Egypt is the gift of the Nile.' He was not wrong.

Every summer, predictably and reliably, the Nile overflowed its banks. When the waters receded, they left behind a thick layer of dark, fertile silt across a strip of land perhaps 15 kilometers wide, cutting through hundreds of kilometers of absolute desert. The ancient Egyptians called this the Kemet — the Black Land. Everything beyond it was the Deshret — the Red Land. Life and death.

This annual flood required management. Early rulers who could answer questions like 'When did it peak this year?' — and organize labor around the answers — gained enormous power.

The result was one of history's first large-scale administrative states. By around 3100 BCE, a ruler known as Narmer unified Upper and Lower Egypt under a single crown. Egypt became a state, and the 3,000-year story of the pharaohs began.

The Nile also made Egypt surprisingly secure. Deserts to east and west, sea to the north, cataracts to the south — natural barriers that kept invaders out and gave Egyptian civilization the stability to develop and persist across 30 dynasties.

chapter_content.image_alt: Satellite image of the Nile Delta and River Valley from space, showing the thin green ribbon of fertile land surrounded by desert

chapter_content.title: The Nile: How Egypt's Sacred River Birthed The Civilization Of Pharaohs | Odyssey

questions.question_content: What did ancient Egyptians call the fertile strip of land left by the Nile floods?
Around what year did Narmer unify Upper and Lower Egypt?
Which ancient historian described Egypt as 'the gift of the Nile'?
Across how many dynasties did Egyptian civilization persist?

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 2: The Great Pyramid — Engineering the Impossible
chapters.chapter_desc: The Great Pyramid remained the tallest structure on Earth for 3,800 years. Built with Bronze Age tools — how?

chapter_content.content:
Let's settle one thing immediately: the pyramids were not built by slaves.

For centuries, European tradition assumed the monuments must have been built through forced labour. But excavations in the 1990s revealed the workers' village beside the Giza plateau — complete with bakeries, breweries, medical facilities, and mass graves showing healed injuries and careful burials. These are people who received care.

The workforce of around 20,000–30,000 skilled workers were fed enormous quantities of meat and bread — we can calculate this from the animal bones and grain storage found in their settlement. They rotated in shifts and received payment.

Now for the engineering. The Great Pyramid of Khufu, completed around 2560 BCE, stands 146 meters tall and contains approximately 2.3 million stone blocks, each weighing 2.5 to 15 tons. The base is level to within 2.1 centimeters across 230 meters. The four sides align to the cardinal directions with an error of less than 0.05 degrees.

With no iron tools and no wheeled vehicles.

The best current understanding combines copper chisels for quarrying, wooden sledges on wetted sand, a workforce organized with military precision, and a system of internal ramps we are still debating today.

The Great Pyramid is a 4,500-year-old message that says simply: We were here. We achieved this.

chapter_content.image_alt: Aerial photograph of the three Giza pyramids with the Sphinx in the foreground

chapter_content.title: Decoding the Great Pyramid | Full Documentary | NOVA | PBS

questions.question_content: What did archaeological excavations in the 1990s reveal about the pyramid builders?

answers.answer_content: They were prisoners of war from Nubia
They were paid workers who received food, care, and were buried respectfully
They were a priestly caste with special religious status
They were slaves bought from Mesopotamian traders

questions.question_content: Approximately how many stone blocks does the Great Pyramid of Khufu contain?
What technique is thought to have reduced friction when moving heavy stone blocks?

answers.answer_content: Rolling the blocks on wooden logs
Pulling blocks on wooden sledges over wetted sand
Floating the blocks on the Nile in barges
Using animal fat as a lubricant on stone ramps

questions.question_content: For how long did the Great Pyramid remain the tallest structure on Earth?

courses.course_description: Explore the most complex structure in the known universe — how your brain forms memories, makes decisions, and occasionally deceives you in fascinating ways.

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 1: 86 Billion Neurons — How Your Brain Is Wired
chapters.chapter_desc: What is the brain actually made of, and how do billions of individual cells produce thought, memory, and consciousness?

chapter_content.content:
Right now, as you read this sentence, approximately 86 billion neurons in your brain are firing electrical signals. Each neuron connects to up to 10,000 others through junctions called synapses. The total number of connections — your brain's 'wiring' — is estimated at 100 trillion.

For comparison: there are about 100 billion stars in the Milky Way. Your brain has roughly the same number of connections as there are stars in 1,000 galaxies.

What actually is a neuron? Picture a tree: a cell body in the middle, long branching 'dendrites' reaching out to receive signals from other cells, and a single long 'axon' sending signals out. When a neuron fires, an electrical pulse travels down the axon at up to 120 meters per second. At the tip, it releases chemical messengers — neurotransmitters — that float across the tiny synaptic gap and trigger the next neuron.

Here is what makes this system extraordinary: it is plastic. Every experience you have changes your brain's physical structure. Neurons that fire together wire together — the synaptic connection between them physically strengthens. This is the mechanism of memory and learning.

When you learn to ride a bike, specific neural pathways strengthen with every fall and correction. When you stop riding for twenty years, those pathways weaken but don't disappear — which is why riding again feels strangely familiar.

chapter_content.image_alt: Microscopy image of neurons in the human brain, showing the cell bodies and intricate web of dendrite connections

chapter_content.title: The Human Brain Explained | Neuroscience Full Documentary

questions.question_content: Approximately how many neurons does the human brain contain?
What are the junctions between neurons called?
What does the phrase 'neurons that fire together wire together' describe?

answers.answer_content: The brain's ability to generate electrical currents
The way synaptic connections strengthen through repeated use — the basis of learning and memory
The way the brain synchronizes left and right hemispheres
The process of neurons dying when they stop being used

questions.question_content: At what speed can an electrical signal travel along a neuron's axon?

answers.answer_content: Up to the speed of light

chapters.chapter_name: Chapter 2: Memory — Why You Remember, Why You Forget, and Why Your Memories Are Wrong
chapters.chapter_desc: Memory is not a recording. It is a reconstruction — and every time you remember something, you subtly change it.

chapter_content.content:
Where were you on September 11, 2001? If you're old enough, you probably have a vivid memory: exactly where you were, who told you, how you felt. Psychologists call these 'flashbulb memories' — moments so emotional they seem burned into the brain with extraordinary clarity.

Here is the disturbing finding: flashbulb memories are wrong. Repeatedly.

In studies conducted after 9/11, researchers asked people to describe their memories in detail — then asked again one, three, and ten years later. The stories changed dramatically. People were confident about details that turned out to be false. One subject was certain they had watched the towers fall on TV at work. In fact, they had been on a plane. They had heard about it when they landed, and the memory had been reconstructed around a scene they later watched on television.

This is not a flaw in unusual people. This is how memory works.

Memory is not a video recording. It is a reconstructive process. Every time you retrieve a memory, you are rebuilding it — drawing on what you encoded, plus your current knowledge, your expectations, and subtle cues in the environment. Then you store that reconstruction. Small errors compound over years.

Real-world implication: eyewitness testimony — long considered the gold standard of criminal evidence — is deeply unreliable. Studies show 70% of wrongful convictions overturned by DNA evidence involved eyewitness misidentification.

chapter_content.image_alt: Diagram of the human brain highlighting the hippocampus, the region critical for forming new long-term memories

chapter_content.title: How Your Memory Works – and Why Forgetting Is Totally OK | TED

questions.question_content: What are 'flashbulb memories'?

answers.answer_content: Vivid memories of highly emotional events that feel permanently and accurately recorded
Memories triggered by a specific smell or taste
Memories that only last for a few seconds

questions.question_content: What does research on flashbulb memories consistently show?

answers.answer_content: They are the most reliable type of memory
They are perfectly accurate if the emotion was strong enough
They change significantly over time despite feeling completely accurate
They fade faster than ordinary memories

questions.question_content: According to the chapter, what is memory best compared to?

answers.answer_content: A video recording that plays back faithfully
A reconstruction that is partially rebuilt each time it is retrieved
A library with fixed and permanent records
A photograph that fades over time but stays accurate

questions.question_content: What percentage of wrongful convictions overturned by DNA evidence involved eyewitness misidentification?
